title: Thursdays With Abie

jokes.text: Oh, I love going to aquatic parks. Sure, they have worse rides than amusement parks, less fish than aquariums- but the parking is ample!
jokes.setup: Homer expressing love for aquatic parks, setting up expectation for standard praise
jokes.punchline: Backhanded compliment ending with ridiculous highlight of parking quality
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer logic - finding the absurd positive in something disappointing. Well-structured buildup to parking punchline.

jokes.text: Oh, there's so many photo ops, but I already filled up my camera in the parking lot. Parking lot C sign, the tram, a license plate from Canada, a bird eating potato chips, my feet walking, that boy who got sick on the tram, Grampa talking to a mermaid hedge, the park rules in Spanish, a fat baby, Grampa telling a story to a trailer hitch. (ding) Oops! I erased them all!
jokes.setup: Marge mentioning she filled up her camera with parking lot photos
jokes.punchline: Increasingly absurd list of mundane/inappropriate photos, capped by accidentally erasing them all
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge characterization - overly enthusiastic documentation of the mundane. Great escalation through the list, perfect deflation with the erasure.

jokes.text: I want to do that! (mechanical whirring) (gasping) I'm going to take stuff from the lost and found! (humming)
jokes.setup: Bart seeing some activity and wanting to participate
jokes.punchline: His enthusiasm leads to immediately planning petty theft from lost and found
jokes.explanation: Classic Bart mischief - takes innocent activity and immediately finds criminal application. Brief but character-true.

jokes.text: My feet hurt! left ear's freezing! My right ear's burning! I got fish smell in my wrinkles!
jokes.setup: Grampa experiencing discomfort at the aquatic park
jokes.punchline: Increasingly specific and gross complaints, ending with fish smell in wrinkles
jokes.explanation: Perfect Grampa - chronic complainer with vivid, gross specifics. Good escalation from general to disgusting.

jokes.text: Hurry! If we don't get a seat in the splash zone, I've worn my bathing suit under my clothes for nothing!
jokes.setup: Urgency about getting seats in the splash zone
jokes.punchline: Reveals she's prepared by wearing bathing suit under clothes, making it seem like wasted effort
jokes.explanation: Classic Marge over-preparation meeting potential disappointment. Economy and character specificity work well.

jokes.text: A splash-'em-up show? Reminds me of the time I high-dived into a damp sponge for the amusement of Federal Reserve Chairman Paul Volcker.
jokes.setup: Grampa hearing about splash show triggering a memory
jokes.punchline: Bizarre specific story involving Federal Reserve Chairman and damp sponge diving
jokes.explanation: Perfect Grampa rambling - starts plausible then veers into specific absurdity with Paul Volcker reference. Great unexpected specificity.

jokes.text: Grampa, no stories! You can come with us, or sit here on the shark bench. You know, I did sit on a shark once... Bathing suit!
jokes.setup: Homer trying to stop Grampa's stories, offering alternative
jokes.punchline: Grampa immediately starts another story despite being told no stories
jokes.explanation: Classic dynamic - Homer trying to control Grampa who completely ignores the request. Sets up running theme of Grampa's unstoppable stories.

jokes.text: Homer sees he's not wearing bathing suit: 'Oops! No bathing suit.'
jokes.setup: Marge's preparation contrasted with Homer's assumption he was prepared
jokes.punchline: Homer's casual discovery of his lack of preparation
jokes.explanation: Simple character moment showing Homer's lack of foresight, contrasting with Marge's over-preparation.

jokes.text: Let's give a wet and wacky welcome to Springfield's oldest performing mollusk... Get to the show! This is the show! Here's the original Octomom: Slimu!
jokes.setup: Announcer introducing the sea show attraction
jokes.punchline: Calling octopus the 'original Octomom' - topical reference to scandal figure
jokes.explanation: Decent topical reference but feels like pop culture name-drop without much additional twist. Gets the job done.

jokes.text: Slimu looks kind of uncomfortable. Wouldn't you be if you were a saltwater fish in a freshwater aquarium?
jokes.setup: Observation about the performing octopus looking uncomfortable
jokes.punchline: Dark reality that the animal is actually suffering in wrong environment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa - pointing out the dark reality behind entertainment. Efficient delivery of uncomfortable truth.

jokes.text: The country has its first black president. Maybe it's time for the first octopus president! And when it comes to signing bills, he can provide his own ink!
jokes.setup: Homer connecting current events to octopus political potential
jokes.punchline: Octopus providing own ink for bill signing - literal interpretation of metaphor
jokes.explanation: Homer's absurd political logic with decent punchline about ink. Timely reference handled with characteristic Homer simplicity.

jokes.text: They'll cheer a dancing octopus, but not an old man complaining about everything.
jokes.setup: Audience cheering for octopus performance
jokes.punchline: Grampa's bitter comparison to his own reception when complaining
jokes.explanation: Perfect Grampa self-awareness and bitterness. Economical and character-specific observation about audience preferences.

jokes.text: Excuse me, sir. Is this seat taken? Well, not for a pretty girl like you! (chuckles nervously)
jokes.setup: Polite inquiry about seat availability
jokes.punchline: Grampa's old-fashioned flirting directed at male journalist
jokes.explanation: Mild cringe humor from Grampa's outdated social habits. Simple misunderstanding but fits character.

jokes.text: Well, this is the first time I ever sat on a shark. Not me-- sat on a real one once!
jokes.setup: Marshall making casual comment about sitting on shark bench
jokes.punchline: Grampa immediately one-ups with claim of sitting on real shark
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup for Grampa's storytelling. Efficient transition from small talk to outrageous claim.

jokes.text: It was back in World War II. I was a Seaman First Class on the USS World War I.
jokes.setup: Grampa starting war story
jokes.punchline: Ship named 'USS World War I' during World War II
jokes.explanation: Clever absurdist detail - ship from previous war serving in current war. Quick, efficient absurdity.

jokes.text: We were minding our own business when we were attacked by a heat-seeking torpedo we had fired the day before.
jokes.setup: Describing enemy attack on their ship
jokes.punchline: Reveals they were attacked by their own torpedo from the day before
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic - heat-seeking torpedo so slow it takes a day to circle back. Great delayed punchline structure.

jokes.text: Don't worry about the sharks, boys. Just play possum and they'll leave you alone. Unfortunately, sharks love possum.
jokes.setup: Grampa giving survival advice about sharks
jokes.punchline: Immediate contradiction that sharks love possum
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection - sets up helpful advice then immediately undermines it with absurd logic. Great punchline timing.

jokes.text: Hey, boys, these sharks can't bite us if we're on their backs! It's like riding the Cyclone back at Coney Island!
jokes.setup: Grampa's shark survival strategy
jokes.punchline: Casual comparison of shark riding to amusement park ride
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of absurd survival story. The casual tone makes the ridiculous situation funnier.

jokes.text: It's like riding a dust devil back in Yuma-- like I read about in a book back in Boston!
jokes.setup: Sailor adding his own comparison to shark riding
jokes.punchline: Reveals his 'experience' is just from reading about it in a book
jokes.explanation: Nice deflation of the sailor's bravado - admits his reference is secondhand knowledge from books.

jokes.text: I still keep in touch with all those men and some of the sharks. Why, here's us in Hawaii last year. Good times.
jokes.setup: Grampa wrapping up his shark story
jokes.punchline: Claims ongoing friendship with the sharks, shows photo of recent vacation with them
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of absurdist story - not only survived sharks but maintains friendships with them decades later. Visual gag of vacation photo.

jokes.text: You're in the newspaper business? (chuckles) Something that's gonna die before I do!
jokes.setup: Learning Marshall is a newspaper columnist
jokes.punchline: Dark observation about newspaper industry decline vs his own mortality
jokes.explanation: Sharp observation about media industry delivered with dark humor about his own advanced age. Economical and witty.

jokes.text: Someone's listening to me! Now I know how a radio feels!
jokes.setup: Grampa excited that journalist wants to hear his stories
jokes.punchline: Comparison to how a radio must feel when people listen
jokes.explanation: Sweet character moment showing Grampa's loneliness and need for attention. Radio comparison is charming if not hilarious.

jokes.text: Oh, not that stupid stuffed lamb again. This isn't learning! Do you want to learn? No, no, no. I'm just saying...
jokes.setup: Bart complaining about school lamb assignment
jokes.punchline: When asked if he wants to learn, immediately backtracks his complaint
jokes.explanation: Classic Bart - complains about school but doesn't actually want to learn either. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: Hey, the only thing I bring home are notes to my parents, and those do not arrive the way that they left.
jokes.setup: Bart explaining why getting the lamb is significant for him
jokes.punchline: Admits he alters disciplinary notes before parents see them
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart confession about tampering with school notes. Economical delivery of his mischievous nature.

jokes.text: Oh, what a nice note! I'm not sticking my hand in that can again.
jokes.setup: Marge reading note, presumably from teacher
jokes.punchline: Refuses to reach into can again, implying previous bad experience
jokes.explanation: Mild visual gag about whatever unpleasant experience Marge had with the can. Setup works better than punchline.

jokes.text: Top of the morning, Homer. Seen the paper? No, I get my news off the Internet. (laughing)
jokes.setup: Ned asking if Homer has seen the newspaper
jokes.punchline: Homer's dismissive response about getting news from internet, delivered with laughter
jokes.explanation: Dated humor about internet vs newspaper. Homer's smug delivery adds character but joke feels generic.

jokes.text: My father is interesting?! The whole world's upside down! That must mean Cathy is funny. Nope.
jokes.setup: Homer shocked that his father could be interesting
jokes.punchline: Tests his upside-down world theory on Cathy comic strip, immediately disproven
jokes.explanation: Great escalation joke with perfect punchline. The Cathy reference is a perfect test case for 'world upside down' theory.

jokes.text: Abe, I never knew you were so full of... (speaking Yiddish)! What does that mean? It's like (speaking Yiddish), but with a pinch more (speaking Yiddish)!
jokes.setup: Ned complimenting Abe using Yiddish word
jokes.punchline: Grampa's explanation using more untranslated Yiddish that doesn't clarify anything
jokes.explanation: Mild absurdist humor about untranslated Yiddish. The circular non-explanation is amusing but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Yep, I may just be the best known Abraham in history.
jokes.setup: Grampa feeling pride about his newfound fame
jokes.punchline: Absurd comparison to Abraham Lincoln
jokes.explanation: Perfect Grampa ego inflation. Economical delivery of his tendency toward grandiose self-importance.

jokes.text: Every Monday, the Tinseltown Starliner would stop at 1:17 p.m. on its way to Hollywood. I never rode that train, but I'd palaver with the movie stars when they got out to stretch their legs.
jokes.setup: Grampa describing his connection to Hollywood glamour
jokes.punchline: Admits he never actually rode the train, just talked to passengers during stops
jokes.explanation: Good setup for deflation - builds expectation of glamorous travel then reveals more modest reality.

jokes.text: Looks like someone has seen my work as an extra in Du Barry, Woman Of Passion. I sure have, Mr. Gable.
jokes.setup: Clark Gable recognizing young Grampa
jokes.punchline: Grampa's proud response about his work as an extra in obscure film
jokes.explanation: Mild deflation joke - Grampa's big Hollywood connection was as an extra in a minor film.

jokes.text: How come you ain't a star yet? Maybe my ears are too big. I don't know.
jokes.setup: Gable asking why Grampa isn't a star
jokes.punchline: Grampa's self-deprecating response about his ears being too big
jokes.explanation: Mild self-deprecating humor that fits the era and characters. Not exceptional but serviceable.

jokes.text: Oh, it's called Gone with the Wind. You can have it. It's only got one swear word, and not a very good one.
jokes.setup: Gable offering to give away a book
jokes.punchline: Dismisses future classic 'Gone with the Wind' for having only one weak swear word
jokes.explanation: Good historical irony - dismissing one of cinema's biggest films for trivial reason. Works on multiple levels.

jokes.text: Well, let's see: garbage, garbage, my line. Garbage, garbage, my line. Atlanta burns, nice dresses. I like it!
jokes.setup: Gable speed-reading through Gone with the Wind script
jokes.punchline: Reduces epic Civil War story to 'garbage, my line' and 'Atlanta burns, nice dresses'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant reduction of epic film to actor's self-centered perspective. Great economy in showing how actors read scripts.

jokes.text: Don't tell this story to anyone for 60 years.
jokes.setup: Gable finishing his interaction with young Grampa
jokes.punchline: Specific timeframe that explains why we're hearing this story now
jokes.explanation: Clever meta-humor explaining the timing of Grampa's story. Economical way to justify the narrative structure.

jokes.text: But his big blue eyes could melt the butter you kept in your pocket for lunch. Lunch butter we called it.
jokes.setup: Grampa describing Clark Gable's attractive eyes
jokes.punchline: Bizarre detail about pocket butter for lunch being a normal thing
jokes.explanation: Perfect Grampa absurdism - starts with normal compliment then veers into bizarre Depression-era detail about 'lunch butter.'

jokes.text: How come Grampa never shared those stories with us? He does all the time. You just never listen.
jokes.setup: Homer wondering why Grampa never told them interesting stories
jokes.punchline: Marge pointing out that he does, Homer just doesn't listen
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - shows Homer's selective attention and Marge's observational clarity. Economical delivery.

jokes.text: Marge, my brain is like a computer, and I file away Grampa's stories in a very special place.
jokes.setup: Homer explaining how he processes Grampa's stories
jokes.punchline: Visual gag of Homer's brain filing stories in trash folder
jokes.explanation: Good setup for visual gag showing Homer's brain literally trashing Grampa's stories. Character-specific humor.

jokes.text: How come you don't get mad when I torment real animals? I do! It enrages me! I thought you Hindus were supposed to love everybody. I'm a freaking Buddhist! A Buddhist!
jokes.setup: Bart questioning why Apu cares about stuffed animal vs real ones
jokes.punchline: Apu's frustrated correction about being Buddhist, not Hindu
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bart's cultural ignorance and Apu's long-suffering patience with corrections.

jokes.text: 'I thought you Hindus were supposed to love everybody.' / 'I'm a freaking Buddhist! A Buddhist!'
jokes.setup: Bart's religious misconception
jokes.punchline: Apu's exasperated correction with unusual profanity
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Apu's frustration with constant misidentification. His uncharacteristic swearing adds impact.

jokes.text: Larry, please don't judge our whole family by Bart. He's the black sheep. I mean, not that black sheep are in any way inferior to white sheep. I do not judge wool by its color-- only by how itchy it is.
jokes.setup: Lisa apologizing to stuffed lamb for Bart's behavior
jokes.punchline: Over-correction about black sheep metaphor leading to absurd statement about wool judgment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa - overthinking and over-correcting to avoid any hint of prejudice, even with metaphors. Great character specificity.

jokes.text: Larry, what sub-genre of jazz do you like? Fusion? Neo-bop? Afro-Cuban?
jokes.setup: Lisa trying to connect with the stuffed lamb
jokes.punchline: Asking stuffed animal detailed questions about jazz preferences
jokes.explanation: Good Lisa characterization - treating stuffed animal like intellectual equal, asking sophisticated questions.

jokes.text: You're coming off desperate, honey.
jokes.setup: Lisa frantically calling for lost stuffed lamb
jokes.punchline: Marge's calm observation about Lisa's desperation
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan observation from Marge. Economical way to comment on Lisa's over-investment in the assignment.

jokes.text: That's how I got my idea for a suitcase with wheels, from watching a commercial about a suitcase with wheels.
jokes.setup: Grampa claiming credit for inventing wheeled suitcase
jokes.punchline: Admits he got the idea from seeing a commercial for already-existing wheeled suitcase
jokes.explanation: Perfect Grampa logic - claiming invention credit while accidentally admitting the product already existed. Great structure.

jokes.text: I'm Mitch Albom, author of Tuesdays with Morrie. Never heard of you. Yeah. Sure you haven't.
jokes.setup: Famous author introducing himself to Grampa
jokes.punchline: Grampa's dismissive response and Albom's disbelief
jokes.explanation: Good meta-humor with real author cameo. Grampa's obliviousness to fame plays well against Albom's expectation.

jokes.text: Take a hike, Hollywood. I've already got a college boy to turn my every word into syndicated gold.
jokes.setup: Marshall dismissing famous author
jokes.punchline: Claiming he's already got someone to make money from Grampa's stories
jokes.explanation: Shows Marshall's possessive attitude toward Grampa and dismissive attitude toward competition.

jokes.text: Clouds are God's sneezes.
jokes.setup: Grampa making philosophical observation
jokes.punchline: Bizarre metaphor comparing clouds to divine sneezing
jokes.explanation: Perfect Grampa non-sequitur. Economical absurdist wisdom that sounds profound but makes no sense.

jokes.text: Well, well, well, look who decided to visit me twice in one year.
jokes.setup: Homer visiting his father
jokes.punchline: Grampa's sarcastic observation about Homer's rare visits
jokes.explanation: Classic guilt-trip humor showing the strained father-son relationship. Economical and character-specific.

jokes.text: This man's more of a son to me than you've ever been. What the...?! Well, he's more of a father to me than you've ever been!
jokes.setup: Grampa praising Marshall as a better son than Homer
jokes.punchline: Marshall's immediate reciprocal claim about Grampa being a better father
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of mutual adoption between Grampa and Marshall. The speed of reciprocal claims is amusing.

jokes.text: It's a father-son commitment band that I bought and never told you about. And now I'm telling you.
jokes.setup: Homer returning a ring to Grampa
jokes.punchline: Reveals it was a commitment ring he bought but never mentioned before this moment
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe moment - Homer's awkward attempt at emotional gesture that he's never mentioned. Good character work.

jokes.text: This isn't Larry. It's just cotton balls on toilet paper rolls.
jokes.setup: Martin opening bag expecting to find stuffed lamb
jokes.punchline: Discovers Bart's crude fake made of bathroom supplies
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag reveal of Bart's desperate attempt to fake the lamb with household items.

jokes.text: Of course I don't, but other people do.
jokes.setup: Questioned about why he cares if he doesn't like the lamb
jokes.punchline: Honest admission that he only cares because others expect him to
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart honesty - doesn't care about the assignment but understands social expectations. Economical character insight.

jokes.text: Nelson loved a little lamb, little lamb, little lamb, Nelson loved a little lamb, He kept me nice and sane.
jokes.setup: Nelson singing variation of 'Mary Had a Little Lamb'
jokes.punchline: Reveals his emotional dependency on the stuffed lamb for mental stability
jokes.explanation: Great character subversion - tough bully Nelson has genuine emotional attachment to stuffed animal. Well-structured song parody.

jokes.text: ABE: And if this is Homer, you're too late for my love.
jokes.setup: Answering machine message from Grampa and Marshall
jokes.punchline: Passive-aggressive message specifically targeting Homer
jokes.explanation: Perfect petty Grampa move - using answering machine for emotional manipulation. Good cringe factor.

jokes.text: Maybe you're not really mad at Grampa. Maybe you're mad at yourself for taking him for granted all these years.
jokes.setup: Homer complaining about Grampa
jokes.punchline: Marge's psychological insight into Homer's real issue
jokes.explanation: Standard sitcom psychological insight moment. Not particularly funny but advances plot and character.

jokes.text: Marge, how can anyone be mad at himself? It's impossible. Watch. Why, you little... (gasps) Oh, I can't. When I look at those beautiful chubby cheeks. Come here, you.
jokes.setup: Homer claiming it's impossible to be mad at yourself
jokes.punchline: Attempts to strangle himself but gets distracted by his own appearance
jokes.explanation: Great Homer logic - tries to prove point through demonstration but gets derailed by self-love. Perfect character moment.

jokes.text: And the life lesson is? The Yangtze River swallows all secrets. Hmm. I'm just gonna put 'Haste makes waste.'
jokes.setup: Burns finishing elaborate story, Marshall asking for moral
jokes.punchline: Burns gives cryptic wisdom, Marshall substitutes generic platitude
jokes.explanation: Perfect contrast between Burns' exotic wisdom and Marshall's need for generic newspaper-friendly moral. Great economy.

jokes.text: Release... a hound. Isn't that cute? He thinks he's a pack.
jokes.setup: Burns using his signature phrase
jokes.punchline: Self-aware comment about having only one hound instead of multiple
jokes.explanation: Good callback that acknowledges the absurdity of Burns' usual phrase when he only has one hound.

jokes.text: Climb that rope! There is no rope. Well, you better get to the ceiling somehow! Ceiling, ceiling, ceiling!
jokes.setup: Teacher ordering student to climb nonexistent rope
jokes.punchline: Insists student reach ceiling anyway, escalating to desperate chanting
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist escalation of unreasonable gym teacher demands. The chanting adds manic energy.

jokes.text: 'Put the rope in your teeth.' / 'What will that do?' / 'It'll shut you up.'
jokes.setup: Milhouse asking for help
jokes.punchline: Bart's dual-purpose solution
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart response - practical advice that also serves his desire to silence Milhouse's complaints. Great economy.

jokes.text: There you are, you piece of crap.
jokes.setup: Bart finding the lost stuffed lamb
jokes.punchline: Greets it with insult despite desperately needing it
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart - can't show genuine affection even when relieved. Economical character beat.

jokes.text: Um, I'm strictly an above-the-ground policeman. What you need is the sewer cops. Fine. What's their number? There's no such thing as the sewer cops. It's just something we tell kids to make them feel safe.
jokes.setup: Wiggum refusing to help with sewer rescue
jokes.punchline: Reveals sewer cops are fictional safety lie for children
jokes.explanation: Great escalation from incompetence to dark revelation. Perfect Wiggum logic and disturbing truth about police lies.

jokes.text: What about Bart? Well, if you miss him, yell into a toilet.
jokes.setup: Lisa asking about her brother trapped in sewer
jokes.punchline: Wiggum's casual suggestion to communicate via toilet
jokes.explanation: Perfect dark Wiggum logic - treats boy's peril as minor inconvenience with absurd solution. Great deadpan delivery.

jokes.text: When I rode with Abe Simpson on the Tinseltown Starliner, I never dreamed his first voyage would be his last? At 3:00 on Monday, Abe passed away peacefully in his sleep.
jokes.setup: Marshall writing about train ride with Grampa
jokes.punchline: Pre-written obituary revealing his murder plan
jokes.explanation: Dark escalation revealing Marshall's true intentions. The pre-written obituary is a genuinely disturbing plot twist.

jokes.text: But would someone really kill a man just to win a Pulitzer Prize?
jokes.setup: Homer questioning the plausibility of murder for journalism award
jokes.punchline: Dramatic music sting answers 'yes'
jokes.explanation: Good setup for dramatic revelation. Homer's innocent question gets ominous musical answer.

jokes.text: Anyone without a ticket will be beaten by classic railroad bulls.
jokes.setup: Train announcer describing final journey
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions violent enforcement by period-appropriate security
jokes.explanation: Mild dark humor about historical railroad violence presented as normal tourist experience.

jokes.text: You don't get a beatin' like this traveling by plane.
jokes.setup: Passenger being beaten by railroad security
jokes.punchline: Positive comparison to modern air travel experience
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor comparing historical train violence favorably to modern airline hassles.

jokes.text: I'm finally riding the Tinseltown Starliner. I could die a happy man.
jokes.setup: Grampa expressing joy about finally riding the train
jokes.punchline: Ironic statement about dying happy given Marshall's murder plan
jokes.explanation: Good dramatic irony - Grampa's innocent comment becomes ominous given audience knowledge of murder plot.

jokes.text: Live each moment as if someone's about to kill you. I mean, as if it's your last. Sorry, my hearing aid's on the fritz. The only word I heard was 'kill.'
jokes.setup: Marshall giving life advice while planning murder
jokes.punchline: Grampa only hearing the word 'kill' due to hearing problems
jokes.explanation: Great dark irony - Marshall's Freudian slip exposes his true intentions, Grampa's hearing problem makes it worse.

jokes.text: They've recreated the '30s. Tent cities, failing banks. No, those are real.
jokes.setup: Homer praising historical recreation
jokes.punchline: Marge revealing current economic problems are real, not recreation
jokes.explanation: Sharp social commentary disguised as simple misunderstanding. Great economy in highlighting economic parallels.

jokes.text: Um, an elephant on a circus train bellowing for his mother.
jokes.setup: Grampa hearing Homer's warning shout
jokes.punchline: Marshall's elaborate cover story for the warning
jokes.explanation: Decent absurdist cover story but not exceptional. The specificity of 'bellowing for his mother' adds some charm.

jokes.text: Larry, you saved my life! You're the most wonderful stuffed lamb in the world. I'm sorry about the mean things I said, and we'll have the rest of the weekend to...
jokes.setup: Bart expressing genuine gratitude to stuffed animal
jokes.punchline: Cut off mid-sentence as lamb is presumably destroyed
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bart's rare vulnerability, cut short by presumed tragedy. Effective emotional setup.

jokes.text: The older I get, the more I like the taste of hot water.
jokes.setup: Grampa making observation about aging
jokes.punchline: Bizarre preference for plain hot water
jokes.explanation: Perfect Grampa observation - sounds wise but is actually bizarre. Economical character-specific humor.

jokes.text: Here comes the prize, Pulitzer Prize, I'll finally be successful in my mother's eyes.
jokes.setup: Marshall approaching sleeping Grampa with murder weapon
jokes.punchline: Singing about winning Pulitzer to impress his mother
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about mother issues driving murder plot. The wedding march melody adds absurd cheerfulness to dark moment.

jokes.text: See you tomorrow at yoga. Jane is sick, so Tricia's filling in. As long as it's not a du!
jokes.setup: Homer and Marshall fighting on train
jokes.punchline: Casual yoga class conversation mid-violence
jokes.explanation: Great juxtaposition of mundane conversation with violent action. The casual tone while fighting is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: How can you stand up to this punishment? I'm the toughest S.O.B. there is-- a biweekly human interest columnist.
jokes.setup: Marshall being questioned about his toughness
jokes.punchline: Claims journalism as ultimate source of resilience
jokes.explanation: Good character humor about journalism being toughening experience. The 'biweekly' detail adds specificity.

jokes.text: Maybe so, but I know how to play possum.
jokes.setup: Grampa responding to being called pathetic
jokes.punchline: References his earlier shark story about playing possum
jokes.explanation: Good callback to earlier story that actually pays off in plot. Economical reference that serves dual purpose.

jokes.text: Just with indifference.
jokes.setup: Grampa saying at least Homer never tried to kill him
jokes.punchline: Homer's honest admission about emotional neglect
jokes.explanation: Perfect economical character moment - Homer's brutal honesty about his emotional neglect. Darkly funny.

jokes.text: It's a ghost quarter, but you'll be able to spend it pretty soon.
jokes.setup: Ghost Gable paying Grampa for old shoeshine
jokes.punchline: Implies Grampa will die soon to use ghost money
jokes.explanation: Creative absurdist concept of ghost currency with dark implication about Grampa's mortality. Original and memorable.

jokes.text: The year is 1946. In a war-torn world, a single flower blooms. And that flower is an angry Japanese monster named Godzilla.
jokes.setup: Homer beginning story with poetic introduction
jokes.punchline: Flower metaphor turns into Godzilla reference
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection from poetic metaphor to monster movie. Perfect Homer storytelling style - starts serious, gets absurd.

jokes.text: Fortunately, there was one man who could help-- Colonel Tom Parker. The Colonel took this monster, cleaned him up and put him onstage under the name The Rolling Stones.
jokes.setup: Homer's story getting more confused
jokes.punchline: Conflates Elvis's manager, Godzilla, and The Rolling Stones
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation of confused cultural references. Homer's complete mix-up of historical figures is perfectly character-consistent.

jokes.text: But then, Godzilla dropped out for a while to do movies, all of the awful movies we see now today in the drive-ins that no longer exist.
jokes.setup: Homer continuing confused entertainment history
jokes.punchline: Blames current bad movies on Godzilla, references extinct drive-ins
jokes.explanation: Continues the absurd story but with less impact. The drive-in reference adds nostalgic detail but doesn't elevate the joke much.

tmdb_overview: Grampa is sitting on a bench waiting for his family when a thoughtful-looking man named Marshall Goldman approaches him and asks to hear about his life. Grampa tells Goldman about the time he was on a World War II battleship, which was hit by an enemy torpedo, and Goldman, a human-interest columnist, publishes it in The Springfield Shopper. Later, Grampa meets Mitch Albom and shares another story with Goldman for a follow-up article. Homer becomes jealous of Grampa's newfound fame and seeks a surrogate father. But when Homer discovers a draft of Goldman's third story, he must race to find Grampa before it is published and his life is changed forever.
